title: Real-World Supervised Learning Examples
concept: Regression and classification power everyday tools across industries: weather forecasting and credit scoring use regression, while email filtering and medical diagnosis use classification.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a label in a supervised learning dataset?

options.text: The correct answer attached to a training example
The model's final prediction
A random guess added for fun

exercises.explain: Right! A label is the correct answer each example is paired with.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Every supervised learning problem is either a regression task or a classification task.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Those are the two task types you've learned this unit — predicting a number, or predicting a category.

cards.title: AI all around you
cards.body: Supervised learning quietly works behind the scenes in weather apps, inboxes, banks, and hospitals. Once you know what to look for, you'll spot regression and classification everywhere.

cards.title: Same two tasks, everywhere
cards.body: Every supervised learning system is doing one of two jobs: regression (predicting a number) or classification (predicting a category) — no matter the industry.

cards.title: Two tools, many jobs
cards.body: Regression and classification are like a tape measure and a sorting tray — the same two tools get reused across totally different jobs, from weather to banking to medicine.

cards.title: Four real examples
cards.body: Weather forecasting predicts tomorrow's temperature — regression. Email filtering sorts messages into spam or inbox — classification. Credit scoring estimates a numeric score like 720 — regression. Medical diagnosis sorts symptoms into a likely condition — classification.

cards.title: It's not always obvious at a glance
cards.body: You might assume anything involving money must be classification (approved or denied) and anything medical must be a category. In reality, a bank might use regression to estimate a numeric credit score, while a hospital might use regression to estimate recovery time in days — the task type depends on the exact question being asked, not the industry.

cards.title: Sorting the four examples
cards.body: Which task type each example uses:

exercises.prompt: A bank predicts a customer's credit score, a number between 300 and 850. What task type is this?

options.text: Regression
Classification
Neither

exercises.explain: Right! A credit score is a number on a scale, so predicting it is a regression task.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Sorting a patient's symptoms into a category like "likely flu" or "likely cold" is classification.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Picking one label from a fixed set of possible conditions is classification.

exercises.sentence: A weather app predicting tomorrow's exact temperature is an example of ___.
exercises.explain: An exact temperature is a continuous number, which makes this regression.

exercises.prompt: Which of these real-world systems is doing classification?

options.text: An email inbox deciding spam vs. not spam
A weather app predicting rainfall in inches
A bank predicting a numeric credit score

exercises.explain: Spam vs. not spam is a choice between two fixed categories — that's classification.

exercises.prompt: A hospital tool estimates how many days a patient will need to recover after a procedure. What task type is this, and why?

options.text: Regression, because recovery days is a number that can vary widely
Classification, because recovery is either "fast" or "slow"
Regression, because hospitals only use numbers
Classification, because days can't be measured precisely

exercises.explain: Exactly! Recovery time in days is a number that can take many values, which is what regression predicts.

exercises.prompt: An insurance company builds two tools: Tool 1 predicts the exact dollar amount to charge a driver for car insurance. Tool 2 decides whether to approve or deny a claim. Which statement correctly identifies each tool's task type?

options.text: Tool 1 is regression (a dollar amount); Tool 2 is classification (approve or deny)
Both tools are regression because they both involve insurance
Both tools are classification because both make a decision
Tool 1 is classification because prices come in categories like "cheap" and "expensive"

exercises.explain: Right! A dollar amount is a number (regression), while approve/deny is a category (classification) — same company, two task types.

exercises.prompt: A farm uses two AI tools: Tool A predicts how many inches of rain will fall this week. Tool B sorts photos of crops into "healthy" or "diseased". A new team member says, "Both tools must be the same task type since they're both about farming." Why is that team member wrong?

options.text: Because the task type depends on whether the output is a number or a category, not on the industry — Tool A is regression and Tool B is classification
Because farming can only use classification
Because rain and crops are actually the same measurement
Because Tool B is also regression since diseases can be counted

exercises.explain: Exactly! The task type comes from the shape of the output — a number or a category — never from the industry the tool is used in.
